data.title: Is Your Strategy Too Narrow For This Market?
data.description:
By Mike Larson

Mike: Are you the kind of investor who focuses mostly on one thing or everything? Well, my next guest is firmly in the latter camp: Fundamentals, technicals, economics, monetary and fiscal policy, and stocks - naturally. But also bonds, currencies, commodities, and more.

If you see the work he and his team put out, you know how comprehensive it is. Now it's time to learn from him what he's seeing in this market and what you should be looking for next. His name is Paul Hickey, and he's co-founder of Bespoke Investment Group. Paul, thanks for joining me today.

So Paul, glad you could join me here. I know I haven't had you on the podcast before, so why don't you talk just a little bit about your background and what it is that you do there at Bespoke.

Paul: Yeah, so I mean, I think what we do at Bespoke - the best way to describe it - is we do a fusion approach to the market, where we look at historicals, we look at quantitative aspects of the market, we look at fundamentals, and we look at technicals. And we put that all together, based on the premise that following solely one discipline toward the market and applying it can lead to errors. But if you look at it from multiple angles and you get agreement across these different angles and disciplines, it can improve your odds of success.

What I learned in my first job out of college - working on an emerging markets trading desk in the late '90s, when Thailand’s currency was imploding and emerging markets were in flux, the US stock market was doing great. But then about four or five weeks later, the news started to leak out that Thailand was falling apart. Not necessarily leak out, but people started paying more attention to it more, and that caused it.

So, we feel like as long as we can look at the market - our primary focus is the equity market - but recognizing that you have to be aware of every aspect of financial markets, geopolitical issues, because they all come back and find their way, price into equities.

Mike: Yeah, I’ll tell you - I look at the research you put out: It’s technicals, fundamentals, overseas, domestic, asset classes, market timing - it’s kind of the whole gamut. So how do you keep track of it all, and is there anything in this market that you think is particularly important for investors to focus on?

Paul: Yeah, so I mean, this is obviously - saying it’s an interesting time would be an understatement. We’ve seen record volatility in April; it’s one of the most volatile months in market history, and that’s really caused some big moves.

But just stepping back a bit - coming into this year, we came off two great years in the market, back-to-back 20% gains. We saw over 50 new highs last year in the S&P 500. On a closing basis, the S&P 500 never closed below its 200-day moving average. All of those are great in the moment, but eventually, those types of environments don’t last forever.

Coming into this year, we didn’t expect the type of volatility we’re seeing now and the type of declines we saw in April. But we were cautioning that gains would be harder to come by because, following prior years we saw that many new highs, like an average of once a week, or we saw the S&P never closing below its 200-day average, forward returns in the next year tended to be weaker than average.

So, it was a little bit of caution. No one expected what happened this year, especially on Liberation Day on April 2nd. So that was a big surprise to markets and investors. The market reacted correctly and swiftly and gave a big smack in the face to the policy of the administration, those, what were called at the time "reciprocal tariffs" - and we're being nice.

Mike: Yeah, it’s funny - I came into this year kind of expecting more volatility, just the way the Trump administration does policy, handles that - not from a political stance, just the surprises all. It’s not the traditional way that Washington does things. It's interesting - I didn’t expect volatility like this. So I'm kind of curious - I think you had a recent TV appearance where you suggested that maybe we may have seen the worst of this post-trade tariff sell-off. I'm curious if you can talk a little more about that. Do you still think that’s the case? And if so, why?

Paul: Yeah, I think so. Again, so this is somewhat of a unique period. We could wake up tomorrow and see tariffs set at 100% for the world - and that would obviously cause markets to fall apart quickly. We’re in this strange moment where just one Truth Social post or tweet can cause problems. So that's something to keep in mind. But looking at the market, people always say, “This time is different,” but in reality the market has been there, done that, and has seen these types of moves - what we think is unique - has seen things similar in the past. So what we’re always doing is listening to the messages of the market.

And getting back to your original question about volatility - we saw a record spike in the VIX, and we saw almost a record decline in the VIX over such short periods of time. Historically, that has usually meant - when you see that decline in the VIX, the worst was over and market returns were positive going forward. Just last week, we saw a back to back to back, a three-peat of 1.5% [daily] gains in the S&P 500 - that's very uncommon. But when you look back historically, about 10 other times - I don't remember the exact number of occurrences - but following those kind of gains, over the next year the S&P was positive again everytime, and it was an average return and a median return of over 20%. So, again, that's another item.

And the lastly, we also saw the Nasdaq 100 breadth was - every stock in the Nasdaq 100 was up one day last week. And that's the type of situation where you see this rushing to buy into the market. It's usually been a sign of gains that are enduring and a rally that will have some endurance to it rather than just a flash in the pan.

Mike: Yeah, well, the Kansas City Chiefs couldn’t get the three-peat done, but it’s good to see the S&P did, right?

Paul: Exactly.

Mike: Let’s talk a little bit about economics, Fed policy and so on. I mean, I’ve seen a lot of headlines thrown out, mostly - you called out one about the Dallas Fed being the worst since COVID. Or this indicator is the worst since GFC, and so on and so forth. Is it just because things were such a shock, or do you think we are going to really have a hard data econonic falloff from what's being going on here?

Paul: Well, so this is what the really fascinating part is about it. You hear this discussion, like you just mentioned... we spoke about the Dallas Fed - the survey-based soft economic data, which has been very weak for months now. And what we've been waiting for is for the soft data to make its way into the hard data - and we haven't quite seen that yet. And so, that’s the divide that most people are expecting to converge. Quite frankly, we would expect some convergence there, but you never know - no one knows the future.

So, what we’re closely monitoring is, right now especially, is jobless claims - which we see every week, and surprisingly they've been pretty firm, we haven't seen a big uptick in the jobless claims. And secondly, with earnings season, we're looking for earnings results. With each earnings report that comes in, we're getting another breadcrumb as to what is going on. It sort of reminds me of back during COVID, when we would watch the case counts updates everyday - and if it was a big uptick in cases the market would sell off, if it was a low number the market would rally. So, when you see these positive earnings reports, the market will rally on it, whereas these negative reports will cause people to be a little bit more downbeat.

So, the results have been surprisingly strong and, obviously, make the argument that that's backward-looking from Q1. What has been surprising to us is, while you've seen a lot of companies pull back guidance, we've seen an equal percentage of companies raise guidance versus lowered guidance. And, I don't know about you, but I wasn't expecting more than a handful of companies to raise guidance this earnings season. What would be the incentive to do so given all the uncertainty out there. So that's something that we can be a little bit optimistic about given what's going on.

But again, the lagged effects of the tariff policy, we're going to start to see that going forward. And if we do see a resolution, if we start to see deals in the coming days, I think the worst of the market declines will certainly be behind us. Because even though we've erased most of April's declines, we were still down about 10% heading into the month.

Mike: That actually leads to the next question. I was going to briefly touch on technicals. You noted something the other day about XLK, the tech sector, was up 11% in five days but was still down 10% YTD. When you look at the technical picture and the health or lack of health in this market, what do you see out there?

Paul: Yeah, I think we’re stuck in a little bit of a no man’s land - between the trenches of the positives and the negatives. We’ve broken some downtrends from the February highs, which is something to be encouraged about, but we’re still below some resistance levels, still below the the 50- and 200-day moving averages.

So we’ve sort of stalled in the last couple of days in the market - not necessarily stalled out, but the S&P as of Tuesday was up six days in a row. So, I think in that resepect it's - the momentum's lowering, but that just has investors questioning, "Oh, have we seen the best?" Because when you've seen the sharp declines of April, you're still a little shell-shocked, so you're on the lookout for anything that can go wrong.

Mike: Alright. Any thoughts on other parts of the market? I mean, obviously gold's been a big story for some people, hitting record highs and having that kind of big reversal and pullback here. Interest rates are in a lot of people's conversations. Any thoughts on either of those kinds of assets as well?

Paul: Yeah, so I think it’s been interesting. Because in the middle of the month we saw equities selling off, and we saw interest rates not going down. And that caused a lot of concerns over Brand America and the attractiveness of US assets.

Our view at the time was that you couldn't base a big trend like that based on two weeks of trading. There were hedge funds positioning that was causing all sorts of flux in the markets. So when you see record volatility in equities, 10% declines over the course of a couple of days, that's going to mess things all up - it’s like throwing a boulder into a pond. You started to see a normalization of the equity market and bonds for the month.

What stands out, though, is the dollar. The dollar has not recovered this month. And for the last two months, it's down close to 8% - which is the type of decline that is, depending on where it finishes the month, it could be the worst two-month drop since December 1987. We haven't seen that kind of move in the dollar in a long time. And in some ways the administration is getting just what it wanted - it wanted a weaker dollar. So that's something to watch. As far as how it impacts the equity market - we were just looking at it this morning - it hasn't really negatively impacted stocks. Forward equity returns of the equity market have actually been slightly above the average, long-term average, when you've seen a 7.5% decline over two months.

Mike: As we get to the tail end of our conversation, any key takeaways? Things that you'd want to highlight - the most important thing or two that somebody could take away here. I mean, any kind of sectors you really like, sectors you don't, stocks you like or don't? Things like that, I'm wondering what all you might be able to offer up.

Paul: Yeah, I think two things to keep in mind. First, as far as the stock basis goes, especially in the case of individual investors looking for the biggest winners - if you look 20 years from now, the biggest winners are not going to be - who know they could be! - but I would bet you just about any amoutn of money that they're not going to be the Mag 7 stocks. Those stocks have seen their gains, they've seen explosive growth. That's not to say they won't do well or they neccessarily have to go down, but the bigger winners are going to be found in other areas where everyone's not focusing. You go back to the 2000 peak - of those stocks, the only one that's really in the top now as it was then is Microsoft (MSFT). All the other top 10 stocks then have underperformed the market by a wide margin. So, you want to broaden your horizons and not just focus on what you're seeing in the papers or on TV everyday.

And the other thing to keep in mind too is, with the biggest winners of any period, and especially for the market too, you want to tune out some of the noise. If I told you the stocks that were going to be the big winners 20 years from now, you still may not have the stomach to withstand the volatility. Like, a stock like Nvidia (NVDA), over the last 20 years, saw at least three drawdowns of over 50%, one drawdown of 90%. For any investor to hold it through that entire period would have been a really difficult endeavor if they were following the market on a day-to-day basis.

Mike: Thanks, Paul - appreciate your time.

Paul: Thanks, Mike. Thanks, everyone.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

data.title: Palantir: The Warning Sign You Must Not Ignore Any Longer (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Palantir: No Room For Disappointment

What a week for investors of Palantir Technologies Inc. (NASDAQ:PLTR), as the leading AI platform company posted its Q1 earnings scorecard. To be honest, I didn’t garner anything amiss from its operating performance that suggests Palantir’s execution has been lacking.

In fact, most, if not all, the metrics have performed admirably, as I shall discuss the salient highlights later, which I believe some of you should be aware of by now. Yet, it’s also an opportune reminder that when expectations are set so high, even a miss on Palantir's forward outlook could engender a post-earnings decline, even though bullish detractors might point out that PLTR nearly “touched” its previous February 2025 highs, marking a stellar recovery from the steep downside experienced over the past two months. Hence, it seems like Palantir CEO Alex Karp’s decision to shelf his planned share sales in March appears to be well-timed, recognizing the potential for the stock to regain its momentum, heading into its Q1 scorecard.

As a reminder, I maintained my bullish rating on PLTR in April. I argued why the stock is well-primed to benefit from the reallocation of defense spending toward high-impact DoD contractors like Palantir, justifying their mission-critical roles in shaping and upgrading the defense technology stack for the US military.

If anything, Palantir's 55% (government) / 45% (commercial) revenue allocation suggests that its defense business should continue to underpin its growth momentum. Meanwhile, it has also managed to accelerate its go-to-market in the enterprise space through its AIP boot camps, affording the leading AI company to showcase to prospective customers with a hands-on approach on how Palantir integrates its prized enterprise data into underlying foundation models.

Hence, I believe it underscores the key value differentiator of Palantir’s ontology, helping the company's platform to avoid being classified and downgraded into one of the more easily commoditized LLM tech.

Palantir: Possible Peak Growth Rates?

Hence, if we refer to Palantir’s salient Q1 highlights, I believe it’s incredibly hard to pinpoint an area of glaring weakness that management must address. Not only has revenue surged in both the government and commercial business, operating margins have also maintained their resilience, reaching 44% in Q1, while translating into a 42% adjusted FCF margin.

Contract value on the commercial business also increased to $810M on a quarterly basis, while the remaining deal value underscores the immense clarity in its outlook, augmenting the underlying momentum that underpins Palantir stock’s highly expensive valuation.

Yet, if you waited until Karp and his team posted their Q1 earnings release and updated their guidance (while Wall Street also upgraded Palantir's near-term estimates), could it have been too late for you to go on board?

Clearly, Palantir’s growth profile suggests a 35% full-year revenue growth rate remains within grasp if they can maintain their strong momentum through the second half of this year.

And the adjusted operating margins could also be kept at the 40%+ level, marking a notable increase from the 30+% zone that underline its profitability evolution over the past two years, helping to garner its GAAP profitability.

Hence, I believe there’s little question that Palantir has demonstrated its prowess, validated its business model, while showcased its execution against less effective AI peers, and also scoring noteworthy wins in the government and enterprise space.

With the US government defense budget seemingly having the potential to surpass the $1T mark in the near term, Palantir’s assessed TAM is arguably increasing as we speak. Moreover, the threat landscape has also evolved markedly, as the US likely appreciates the need to overhaul its contracting landscape to involve more capable defense startups in its sourcing programs, potentially helping the US to close the gap against China’s rapidly improving threat capabilities.

The current geopolitical landscape is potentially splitting into multipolar factions dominating their respective spheres of influence. I believe it’s arguable that US will face an increasingly dominant China that wants to assert its right to “rightful” role in global leadership and dominance, suggesting a global AI arms race to define their military superiority becomes not only critical, but also imperative for the US to keep China’s ambitions at bay.

Hence, Palantir’s role in helping the US shape its leadership role within its geopolitical sphere of influence will be increasingly pivotal and influential, not less, suggesting that a premium valuation isn’t just plausible, but also deserved for PLTR stock. However, the question about how much is too much remains the most pertinent and arduous question that all Palantir investors must search deeply within to contemplate their response.

Buy Or Sell PLTR Stock?

Bearish prognosticators can be forgiven for arguing that it seems like 2021 all over again as PLTR surged above the 160x forward EBITDA multiple in early 2025. We briefly touched that level last week, although the downside volatility this week saw a brief retracement to the 138x level as I pen this update.

In other words, it does seem like there’s a stiff resistance as PLTR buyers have failed to advance past the $125 level for 2025, with selling resistance robustly denying the fervent bulls committed to break past the level to justify a further advance.

Also, there are already signs of a PLTR double top bull trap that I’ve observed. The signal was also augmented by Wall Street technical analysts who bothered to pay heed to the warning signs and not just stay “blindly” bullish on PLTR’s momentum, notwithstanding its remarkable execution and upside bias. Let me show you why.

It’s clear that PLTR has maintained its uptrend bias and is reigniting a tremendous recovery from its April bear market. Yet, the rapid “flush-up” price action in the past four weeks is reminiscent of typical “vertical-esque” formations that typified bull traps (false upside breakout) that I’ve often observed in the past.

While history may not always repeat itself, it often rhymes, and even renowned investors such as Howard Marks have also informed us to learn how to engage in pattern recognition to identity incidences of plausible extreme bullishness and extreme bearishness.

In this instance, I gleaned that not only did the sharp surge over the past four weeks epitomize extreme bullishness into well-overvalued zones discussed earlier, it also met with a stiff resistance (again) just under the $125 zone. The bearish price action that we saw this week (although the week is not over yet) has raised the signal for increased caution, suggesting that PLTR could be tipping over if the bearish action is validated by the end of this week.

In other words, I believe that the market action is in sync with Wall Street’s estimates that Palantir might be close or plausibly has already reached peak growth rates in 2025.

Unless management takes its guidance to the next level higher, I believe the market may not align with the bullish fervor that saw PLTR peak out at the $125 level. The selling resistance that has stymied further buying advances past that level could stupefy late buyers, who think that somehow the market could be fooled into paying for even higher valuations while contemplating the possibility of a growth deceleration from next year.

As a result, I believe that holding a bullish view at this level is increasingly tenuous. Investors with sizable individual stock allocations to PLTR might also want to consider lowering their exposure to PLTR.

Rating: Downgrade to Hold.

data.title: Franklin Floating Rate Daily Access Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: There was a significant swing in financial market sentiment across the first quarter of 2025 as participants digested the first few months of US President Donald Trump’s new administration’s policies. Although there was no change in the fed funds rate, US Federal Reserve policymakers now expect materially slower growth, higher unemployment and higher inflation this year.
  • Detractors: Security selection in information technology, health care and diversified media issuers. Allocation to equity received from prior restructurings and cash exposure. Overweight loan allocation in the retail industry. Underweight loan allocation in the telecommunications segment. Overweight allocation in middle-tier loans.
  • Contributors: Security selection in service, chemical and food issuers. Underweight loan allocation in the housing industry. Overweight loan allocation in the financial segment.
  • Outlook: Overall, we believe that the loan market is in a better position than it was a few years ago, with a manageable maturity wall and issuers having confronted and mitigated balance sheet issues when possible over the last few years.

Performance Review

During the quarter, our overweight loan allocation in the retail industry and underweight loan allocation in the telecommunications segment hindered results. Conversely, our underweight loan allocation in the housing industry and overweight loan allocation in the financial segment contributed to relative performance. Our allocation to equity received from prior restructurings hindered performance, as did our cash exposure.

Our overall loan selection detracted from results, led by selection in information technology, health care and diversified media issuers. Conversely, our selection in service, chemical and food issuers benefited performance.

The fund’s overweight allocation in middle-tier loans relative to the index hindered results.

Outlook

During March, the bank loan market receded somewhat from tight levels, as investors and issuers took a step back to assess the likely impacts from policy announcements. Overall though, technical conditions remained supportive in the form of healthy collateralized loan obligation (CLO) demand and muted new issue supply. Issuers continued to be nimble in accessing the high-yield, private debt and loan markets for the best financing terms. However, activity slowed down materially in the last week of the month as investors awaited tariff announcements from President Trump’s administration.

Since then, loan prices have not been immune to Trump’s “Liberation Day” announcements after quarter-end. Supply and demand have pulled back materially, with the secondary market trading down, CLO liability spreads widening out, and retail funds seeing outflows. As a partial offset, new issue loan supply has dwindled to minimal levels. We see investors being diligent about identifying opportunities in the secondary market and, more importantly, assessing portfolio risks in issuers that will have an outsized impact from an admittedly currently murky tariff picture. On the fundamentals front, we are awaiting first-quarter earnings from loan issuers and will be watching out for changes in forward guidance.

Overall, we believe that the loan market is in a better position than it was a few years ago, with a manageable maturity wall and issuers having confronted and mitigated balance sheet issues when possible over the last few years. We also believe that further stimulative policy announcements from the administration in the form of tax cuts and/or business deregulation will provide some offset to the pressures that heightened tariffs will create. In this environment of heightened uncertainty, we remain focused on the underlying fundamentals of issuers over the medium term. We do expect that bifurcation will persist as some industries and issuers will be disproportionately impacted by tariff implementations. We therefore remain focused on diligent security selection.

Performance data quoted represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the figures shown. Principal value and investment returns will fluctuate, and investors’ shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. Performance would have been lower if fees had not been waived in various periods. Total returns assume the reinvestment of all distributions and the deduction of all fund expenses. Returns with sales charge reflect a deduction of the stated maximum sales charge. An investor cannot invest directly in an index, and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges. Returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized. All classes of shares may not be available to all investors or through all distribution channels. For current month-end performance, please visit Mutual Funds | ETFs | Insights.

Gross expenses are the fund’s total annual operating expenses as of the fund’s prospectus available at the time of publication. Actual expenses may be higher and may impact portfolio returns. Net expenses reflect contractual fee waivers, expense caps and/or reimbursements, which cannot be terminated prior to 02/28/2026 without Board consent. Additional amounts may be voluntarily waived and/or reimbursed and may be modified or discontinued at any time without notice.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. Fixed income securities involve interest rate, credit, inflation and reinvestment risks, and possible loss of principal. As interest rates rise, the value of fixed income securities falls. The fund should not be considered an alternative to money market funds or certificates of deposit (CDs). Floating-rate loans and debt securities are typically rated below investment grade and are subject to greater risk of default, which could result in loss of principal. Low-rated, high-yield bonds are subject to greater price volatility, illiquidity and possibility of default. Changes in the credit rating of a bond, or in the credit rating or financial strength of a bond’s issuer, insurer or guarantor, may affect the bond’s value. Active management does not ensure gains or protect against market declines. The manager may consider environmental, social and governance criteria in the research or investment process; however, ESG considerations may not be a determinative factor in security selection. In addition, the manager may not assess every investment for ESG criteria, and not every ESG factor may be identified or evaluated. These and other risks are discussed in the fund’s prospectus.

Glossary

A collateralized loan obligation (CLO) is a single security backed by a pool of debt.

Important Information

Effective March 1, 2024, the fund changed its benchmark to the Morningstar LSTA US Leveraged Loan Index.

The information provided is not a complete analysis of every material fact regarding any country, market, industry, security or fund. Because market and economic conditions are subject to change, comments, opinions and analyses are rendered as of the date of this material and may change without notice. A portfolio manager’s assessment of a particular security, investment or strategy is not intended as individual investment advice or a recommendation or solicitation to buy, sell or hold any security or to adopt any investment strategy; it is intended only to provide insight into the fund’s portfolio selection process. Holdings are subject to change.

The Morningstar LSTA US Leveraged Loan Index is designed to reflect the performance of the largest facilities in the leveraged loan market. This fund is not sponsored, endorsed, sold or promoted by Morningstar, Inc. or any of its affiliates.

Important data provider notices and terms available at www.franklintempletondatasources.com. All data is subject to change.

Effective Duration is a duration calculation for bonds with embedded options. Effective duration takes into account that expected cash flows will fluctuate as interest rates change. Duration measures the sensitivity of price (the value of principal) of a fixed-income investment to a change in interest rates. The higher the duration number, the more sensitive a fixed-income investment will be to interest rate changes. The 30-day SEC yield is calculated using the net income (interest and dividends) per share earned over a trailing 30-day period (annualized), divided by the fund’s share price at the end of that period. It may not equal the fund’s actual income distribution rate, which reflects the fund’s past dividends paid to shareholders.

Credit Quality is a measure of a bond issuer’s ability to repay interest and principal in a timely manner. The credit ratings shown are based on each portfolio security’s rating as provided by Standard and Poor’s, Moody’s Investors Service and/or Fitch Ratings, Ltd. and typically range from AAA (higheSt) to D (lowest), or an equivalent and/or similar rating. For this purpose, the manager assigns each security the middle rating from these three agencies. When only two agencies provide ratings, the lower of the two ratings will be assigned. When only one agency assigns a rating, that rating will be used. Foreign government bonds without a specific rating are assigned a country rating, if available. Securities that are unrated by all three agencies are reflected as such. The credit quality of the investments in the Fund’s portfolio does not apply to the stability or safety of the Fund. These ratings are updated monthly and may change over time. Please note, the Fund itself has not been rated by an independent rating agency.

Franklin Distributors, LLC. Member FINRA/SIPC.

© Franklin Templeton. All rights reserved.

Not FDIC Insured | No Bank Guarantee | May Lose Value

data.title: Nutrien Q1 2025 Earnings: Expect More Near-Term Pressure (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Nutrien Ltd. (NYSE:NTR) just reported its Q1 2025 results and the initial negative market reaction is justified given the somewhat lacklustre results.

At the same time, the stock has been performing exceptionally well in recent months - outperforming both some of its peers and the broader equity market as well. Being a high beta stock, NTR was supposed to fall sharply in recent months as the S&P 500 retraced from its all-time highs, but the opposite has happened.

This performance suggests that the stock has likely reached a bottom in late 2024 when short-term pessimism has resulted in a mismatch between the share price and actual business fundamentals.

There's also increasing probability that the recent bear market in key agricultural commodities is now over and with that the average net selling price of NTR's fertilizers has already hit rock-bottom.

With that in mind, it is important to sift through the quarterly noise from the recent earnings release and then assess Nutrien's current valuation by taking into account ongoing business performance.

A Disappointing Quarter

In a nutshell, the first quarter of 2025 was nothing to be excited about. Nutrien missed consensus estimates, both on the top and bottom line as weather-related delays had a negative impact on Retail sales while potash average net selling price continued to decline in North America.

Full year guidance was reiterated, but that wasn't enough to appease investors who seem to have expected this quarterly release to mark a long-awaited turnaround for company's earnings.

Instead, sales remained at record lows and the company was barely profitable on a GAAP basis, with adjusted earnings per share falling by 76% year-on-year.

Even though the first quarter is not of seasonal importance to the same extent that the second one is, the sharp fall of gross margins to a record-low is foreshadowing profitability headwinds through the rest of the year.

Overall, the quarter was disappointing and the likelihood of NTR share price continuing on its ascent in the coming months has been diminished.

While NTR remains very conservatively priced on a long-term basis, the share price seems to have been running ahead of itself in recent months as the Price/Sales multiple has just recently reached levels last seen in early 2023.

More Near-Term Pressure

As average net selling price in Potash continued to decline and Nutrien also faced cost headwinds from higher natural gas prices, the company's gross margin reached a new low. The drop on a trailing-twelve months is not substantial, if we ignore the Phosphate segment, however it does not bode well for expectations for the rest of 2025.

Higher volumes and lack of certain one-off items within the retail segment are what's expected to support earnings through the rest of 2025 and allow Nutrien's management to achieve their full fiscal year guidance.

2025 full-year guidance ranges have been maintained and reflect expectations for growth in upstream fertilizer volumes, higher downstream Retail earnings and lower capital expenditures.

Source: NTR Q1 2025 Earnings Release

So far, there's nothing to suggest that Nutrien is no-longer a long-term buy, but the share price is likely to face more pressure in the coming months.

Last year, I used the relationship between the company's return on equity and the Price/Book multiple as a reason to justify my rating upgrade. Following the yesterday's earnings release, however, the two variables have now gone in different directions and gap between the two has widened. Therefore, unless there is an exogenous shock that results in higher fertilizer prices, I expect NTR to face more near-term pressure.

Nutrien's free cash flow has also fallen sharply during the last quarter as profitability headwinds were exacerbated by working capital outflows. And while the latter is could reverse through the rest of the year, it seems highly likely that NTR would continue to trade at a free cash flow yield that is significantly below the current yield on U.S. Treasuries.

At the same time, capital expenditure remains very low and is currently below the sanitary minimum of the company's annual depreciation expense. This is not a cause of concern as long as the Capex to Depreciation ratio does not remain below 100% for a longer period of time. Nevertheless, it is a sign that the current level of capital expenditure is unsustainable beyond the short-term.

Conclusion

Nutrien's strategic competitive positioning remains unchanged and with that my initial investment thesis remains unchanged. Having said that, exceptionally strong price performance in recent months, in combination with the disappointing quarterly results are now likely to result in more downward pressure on the stock price over the coming months.

data.title: Franklin California High Yield Municipal Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: The municipal bond market, as measured by the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index, posted a slightly negative result for the first quarter of 2025 (Q1). January and February saw positive returns supported by healthy inflows of funds and steady demand, while returns posted in March reversed those gains.
  • Contributors: Overweight allocation to muni bonds with no external credit rating, underweight allocation to AA rated bonds and security selection in AA rated bonds.
  • Detractors: Overweight allocation to muni bonds with 20 or more years to maturity, underweight allocation to muni bonds with two to five years to maturity, underweight allocation to AAA rated bonds and security selection in A rated bonds.
  • Outlook: Potential policy changes under US President Donald Trump’s administration, such as the possible elimination of tax exemptions for certain bonds and the cancellation of federal leases, are adding uncertainty to the muni market outlook, alongside broader concerns about how federal decisions may impact local economies.

Performance Review

The fund’s yield curve positioning detracted from relative performance during the quarter, primarily driven by our overweight to muni bonds with 20 or more years to maturity and underweight to muni bonds with two to five years to maturity.

Outlook

There was a significant swing in market sentiment across Q1 as participants digested the first few months of the new Trump administration. January saw positive excess returns across a number of sectors as market sentiment moved higher in anticipation of new business friendly initiatives. This was replaced by a negative shift in risk appetite during February and March as unclear tariff policies became the market’s focus. Throughout the quarter, investment-grade munis underperformed US Treasuries. Despite modest outflows in March, muni funds overall benefited from steady inflows earlier in the quarter.

Muni credit fundamentals remain broadly stable, with signs of improvement even in sectors that struggled post-pandemic, though revenue growth is moderating, and economic tailwinds are slowing. High reserves and fiscal flexibility continue to support most issuers, and credit ratings are expected to remain steady over the next six months, though sector-specific challenges persist.

Potential policy changes under the Trump administration, such as the possible elimination of tax exemptions for certain bonds and the cancellation of federal leases, are adding uncertainty to the muni market outlook, alongside broader concerns about how federal decisions may impact local economies. At the same time, US consumer sentiment is softening, and overall uncertainty around the economic growth outlook has increased in response to the administration’s proposed reforms and policy direction.

However, hard data continues to indicate a resilient US economy. The labor market appears to have reached an equilibrium of a low level of both new additions and layoffs, with a still-low unemployment rate, and economic activity remains solid.

Going forward, our economists are looking to deregulation and tax reforms to support growth over the medium term. The heightened uncertainty surrounding the administration’s trade policy and the impact on US businesses makes US Federal Reserve policy difficult to predict. It remains to be seen whether risks are skewed toward a significant slowdown in growth or higher inflation. However, our economists remain optimistic on US growth, considering that the economy remains on solid footing and pro-growth measures are still expected. Consequently, we are penciling in only one 25-basis point rate reduction in 2025, though an absence of rate cuts this year is also possible.

Overall Rating as of March 31, 2025

As of 03/31/2025 the fund’s Advisor Class shares received a 5 star and Class A shares received a 4 star overall Morningstar rating™, measuring risk-adjusted returns against 100, 97 and 77 Muni California Long funds over the 3-, 5- and 10- year periods, respectively. A fund’s overall rating is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5- and 10-year (if applicable) rating metrics.

Benchmark(s)

Benchmark =Bloomberg Municipal Bond California Exempt Index

Performance data quoted represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the figures shown. Principal value and investment returns will fluctuate, and investors’ shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. Performance would have been lower if fees had not been waived in various periods. Total returns assume the reinvestment of all distributions and the deduction of all fund expenses. Returns with sales charge reflect a deduction of the stated maximum sales charge. An investor cannot invest directly in an index, and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges. Returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized. All classes of shares may not be available to all investors or through all distribution channels. For current month-end performance, please visit Mutual Funds | ETFs | Insights.

The fund began offering Advisor Class shares on 11/15/2006 and the fund began offering A Class shares on 9/10/2018. Performance quotations have been calculated as follows: (A) for Advisor Class periods prior to 11/15/2006, a restated figure is used based on the fund’s Class A1 performance; for A Class periods prior to 9/10/2018, a restated figure is used based on the fund’s Class A1 performance. The performance was adjusted to take into account differences in class-specific operating expenses and maximum sales charges. (b) For periods after share class offering, performance for the specific share class is used, reflecting the expenses and maximum sales charges applicable to that class.

Gross expenses are the fund’s total annual operating expenses as of the fund’s prospectus available at the time of publication. Net expenses are capped under a contractual agreement, which cannot be terminated prior to 06/30/2025 without Board consent. Actual expenses may be higher and may impact portfolio returns.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. Fixed income securities involve interest rate, credit, inflation and reinvestment risks, and possible loss of principal. As interest rates rise, the value of fixed income securities falls. Portfolios focused on a single state are subject to greater risk of adverse economic and regulatory changes in that state than a geographically diversified fund. Changes in the credit rating of a bond, or in the credit rating or financial strength of a bond’s issuer, insurer or guarantor, may affect the bond’s value. Low- rated, high-yield bonds are subject to greater price volatility, illiquidity and possibility of default. An investor may be subject to the federal Alternative Minimum Tax, and state and local taxes may apply. These and other risks are discussed in the fund’s prospectus.

Glossary

A basis point (bp, or bps) is one one-hundredth of one percent (1/100% or 0.01%).

The Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index is a broad measure of the municipal bond market with maturities of at least one year. Source: Bloomberg Indices. The yield curve shows the relationship between yields and maturity dates for a similar class of bonds.

Important Information

The information provided is not a complete analysis of every material fact regarding any country, market, industry, security or fund. Because market and economic conditions are subject to change, comments, opinions and analyses are rendered as of the date of this material and may change without notice. A portfolio manager’s assessment of a particular security, investment or strategy is not intended as individual investment advice or a recommendation or solicitation to buy, sell or hold any security or to adopt any investment strategy; it is intended only to provide insight into the fund’s portfolio selection process. Holdings are subject to change.

The Bloomberg California Municipal Bond Exempt Index is a market value-weighted index of California investment-grade fixed-rate municipal bonds with maturities of one year or more. Source: Bloomberg Indices.

Important data provider notices and terms available at www.franklintempletondatasources.com. All data is subject to change.

Effective Duration is a duration calculation for bonds with embedded options. Effective duration takes into account that expected cash flows will fluctuate as interest rates change. Duration measures the sensitivity of price (the value of principal) of a fixed-income investment to a change in interest rates. The higher the duration number, the more sensitive a fixed-income investment will be to interest rate changes. The 30-day SEC yield is calculated using the net income (interest and dividends) per share earned over a trailing 30-day period (annualized), divided by the fund’s share price at the end of that period. It may not equal the fund’s actual income distribution rate, which reflects the fund’s past dividends paid to shareholders.

Morningstar Rating™: Source: Morningstar®, 03/31/2025. For each mutual fund and ETF with at least a 3-year history, Morningstar calculates a Morningstar Rating™ based on how a fund ranks on a Morningstar Risk-Adjusted Return measure against other funds in the same category. This measure takes into account variations in a fund’s monthly performance, and does not take into account the effects of sales charges and loads, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The top 10% of funds in each category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The weights are: 100% 3-year rating for 36-59 months of total returns, 60% 5-year rating/40% 3-year rating for 60-119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% 5-year rating/20% 3-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent 3-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods. The fund’s Advisor Class shares received a Morningstar Rating of 5, 5 and 5 and fund’s Class A shares received a Morningstar Rating of 4, 4 and 5 star(s) for the 3-, 5- and 10-year periods, respectively. Franklin Templeton provides this fund’s Morningstar Rating™ for Advisor Class and Class A shares only. Other share classes may have different Morningstar ratings.
